coredata.dc:title: Stimulus responsive nanogel with innate near IR fluorescent capability for drug delivery and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Vijayan, Vineeth M.
Shenoy, Sachin J.
Victor, Sunita P.
Muthu, Jayabalan
coredata.dc:description: A brighter, non toxic and biocompatible optical imaging agent is one of the major quests of biomedical research. Here in, we report a photoluminescent comacromer [PEG-poly(propylene fumarate)-citric acid-glycine] and novel stimulus (pH) responsive nanogel endowed with excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence (EDF) for combined drug delivery and bioimaging applications. The comacromer when excited at different wavelengths in visible region from 400nm to 640nm exhibits fluorescent emissions from 510nm to 718nm in aqueous condition. It has high Stokes shift (120nm), fluorescent lifetime (7 nanoseconds) and quantum yield (50%). The nanogel, C-PLM-NG, prepared with this photoluminescent comacromer and N,N-dimethyl amino ethylmethacrylate (DMEMA) has spherical morphology with particle size around 100nm and 180nm at pH 7.4 (physiological) and 5.5 (intracellular acidic condition of cancer cells) respectively. The studies on fluorescence characteristics of C-PLM NG in aqueous condition reveal large red-shift with emissions from 523nm to 700nm for excitations from 460nm to 600nm ascertaining the EDF characteristics. Imaging the near IR emission with excitation at 535nm was accomplished using cut-off filters. The nanogel undergoes pH responsive swelling and releases around 50% doxorubicin (DOX) at pH 5.5 in comparison with 15% observed at pH 7.4. The studies on in vitro cytotoxicity with MTT assay and hemolysis revealed that the present nanogel is non-toxic. The DOX-loaded C-PLM-NG encapsulated in Hela cells induces lysis of cancer cells. The inherent EDF characteristics associated with C-PLM NG enable cellular imaging of Hela cells. The studies on biodistribution and clearance mechanism of C-PLM-NG from the body of mice reveal bioimaging capability and safety of the present nanogel. This is the first report on a polymeric nanogel with innate near IR emissions for bioimaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Excitation dependent fluorescence
Near IR
Nanogel
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Experimental
Materials
Preparation of stimuli responsive self fluorescent nanogel
Swelling and morphology of gels
Determination of photoluminescent properties of C-PLM and its gels
In vitro drug loading and release profiles of nanogel
Evaluation of cytotoxicity of the nanogel
Hemolysis test
Evaluation of cellular uptake of the nanogel
Studies on in vivo bioimaging and biodistribution in mice
Statistical analysis
Results and discussion
Preparation and characterisation of modified photoluminescent comacromer (C-PLM)
Analysis of photoluminescence of the C-PLM comacromer
Studies of photoluminescence of the C-PLM based hydrogel and nanogel
Swelling and morphology of gels
Evaluation of cytocompatibility, hemocompatibility, drug release and cellular uptake of nanogel
In vivo fluorescence bioimaging studies on mice
Conclusions
Acknowledgments
References

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of photoluminescent C-PLM comacromer and its crosslinking with N,N-Dimethyl amino ethylmethacrylate (DMEMA) to get gel..
Spectral analyses of C-PLM comacromer. FTIR spectra (a). 1H NMR spectra (b).

Photoluminescence properties of C-PLM comacromer. The EDF spectra of C-PLM comacromer under aqueous conditions at different excitations on visible region (400nm⿿640nm) (a). Photoluminescence life time of C-PLM comacromer (λext: 440nm & λemiss: 540nm) (b). Photostability graph of C-PLM (λext: 420nm & λemiss: 525nm) (c). Fluorescent quantum yield measurements of C-PLM (d). Excitation & emission spectra of C-PLM comacromer depicting Stoke⿿s shift (e).

Formation of fluorescent hydrogel (C-PLM-HG) from C-PLM comacromer and corresponding fluorescent Geldoc image of C-PLM-HG (a). Solid state photoluminescent spectra of C-PLM-HG (b). EDF spectra of C-PLM-NG under aqueous conditions at different excitations on visible region (460nm⿿600nm) (c). IVIS in vitro optical images of C-PLM-NG exhibiting EDF behavior (excitation and emission wavelengths detected with cut off filters are denoted at the bottom of each image) (d). Variation of swelling (%) of C-PLM-HG at different pH. Data represents Mean±SD (n=3) (e). Morphology and size of nanogel C-PLM-NG influenced by the pH of the medium, at pH 7.4 (i) at pH 5.5 (ii) (f). Dynamic light scattering measurement of C-PLM-NG at pH 7.4 (i) at pH 5.5 (ii) (g).

MTT assay showing the cell viability of the C-PLM-NG at different concentrations (500⿿6000μg/ml) (a). Drug release profile of doxorubicin from C-PLM-NG at different pH (b). Cell viability of Hela cells incubated with different concentrations of C-PLM-NG and doxorubicin-loaded C-PLM-NG (c). Fluorescent microscope images of C-PLM-NG uptake in Hela cell line captured using FITC filter and Texas red filter (The scale bar inside the images represents 50μm),data represents Mean±SD (n=3) (d).

Images of the subcutaneously injected nanogel on mice for exciations at 430nm, 465nm and 505nm (a) (The circled area shows the position of injected area). Processed images of the subcutaneously injected nanogel for exciation at 535nm and imaging with cut off filters (b). Biodistribution of intravenously injected nanogel in mice (c) (Inset shows the excitation and emission wavelengths detected with cut off filters).

Ex vivo imaging of organs of mice after 3h and 24h post injection depicting biodistribution (a) (Inset shows the excitation and emission wavelengths detected with cut off filters). H&E stained images of organs of mice at 24h and 48h post injection (b).
Lymph node imaging of a mice using C-PLM-NG (circled area shows the position of Lymph node) (Inset shows the excitation and emission wavelengths detected with cut off filters).

head.ce:title: Stimulus responsive nanogel with innate near IR fluorescent capability for drug delivery and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology, Polymer Science Division, BMT Wing, Thiruvananthapuram 695012, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology, Division of In vivo models and Testing, BMT Wing, Thiruvananthapuram 695012, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences and Technology
Division of In vivo models and Testing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A brighter, non toxic and biocompatible optical imaging agent is one of the major quests of biomedical research. Here in, we report a photoluminescent comacromer [PEG-poly(propylene fumarate)-citric acid-glycine] and novel stimulus (pH) responsive nanogel endowed with excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence (EDF) for combined drug delivery and bioimaging applications. The comacromer when excited at different wavelengths in visible region from 400nm to 640nm exhibits fluorescent emissions from 510nm to 718nm in aqueous condition. It has high Stokes shift (120nm), fluorescent lifetime (7 nanoseconds) and quantum yield (50%). The nanogel, C-PLM-NG, prepared with this photoluminescent comacromer and N,N-dimethyl amino ethylmethacrylate (DMEMA) has spherical morphology with particle size around 100nm and 180nm at pH 7.4 (physiological) and 5.5 (intracellular acidic condition of cancer cells) respectively. The studies on fluorescence characteristics of C-PLM NG in aqueous condition reveal large red-shift with emissions from 523nm to 700nm for excitations from 460nm to 600nm ascertaining the EDF characteristics. Imaging the near IR emission with excitation at 535nm was accomplished using cut-off filters. The nanogel undergoes pH responsive swelling and releases around 50% doxorubicin (DOX) at pH 5.5 in comparison with 15% observed at pH 7.4. The studies on in vitro cytotoxicity with MTT assay and hemolysis revealed that the present nanogel is non-toxic. The DOX-loaded C-PLM-NG encapsulated in Hela cells induces lysis of cancer cells. The inherent EDF characteristics associated with C-PLM NG enable cellular imaging of Hela cells. The studies on biodistribution and clearance mechanism of C-PLM-NG from the body of mice reveal bioimaging capability and safety of the present nanogel. This is the first report on a polymeric nanogel with innate near IR emissions for bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence bioimaging is one of the prime imaging modalities used for disease diagnostics due to its superior characteristics such as high sensitivity, high selectivity, diversity and nondestructive nature [1,2]. Recently fluorescent nanostructures such as fluorescent proteins, organic dyes, quantum dots, up conversion nanophosphors, polymer dots and gold nanoclusters are getting increased attention in biomedical imaging [3⿿8]. Among them, fluorescent nanostructures such as quantum dots and Pdot capable of giving multiple emissions have also emerged as promising materials for bioimaging [9]. They possess superior properties such as high sensitivity, signal stability, multiplexing capability, high photostability, and higher quantum yield and large stokes shift [10⿿12]. However, shortcomings such as aggregation in biological media and rapid elimination from the body limit the practical utility of these fluorescent probes [13]. The toxicity of different fluorophores could be alleviated by linking them with polymer [14]. Moreover, in comparison with small fluorophore molecules, polymer-based fluorescent nanostructures have some merits such as dispersibility in aqueous media, photostability, emission switchability, biocompatibility, stimuli-responsiveness and accumulation of therapeutic and/or diagnostic agent into target tissues. Organic dyes and quantum dots conjugated with polymers viz. carbocyanine dye with PEG-PCL micelles, CdSe-ZnS quantum dots with PLGA, phenylboronic acid dye with polylactic acid, CdTe QD conjugated with polycarbonate urea and fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) with PLA have been reported [15⿿19]. However, the recent study on the tri-n-octylphosphine oxide-poly(maleic anhydride-alt-1-tetradecene) (TOPO-PMAT) coated CdSe/ZnS Qdots revealed significant pulmonary toxicity to mice model [20].

Nanostructures based on Ͽ-conjugated polymer functionalized with ionic side-chains such as carboxylate, sulfate and quaternary ammonium group, polyfluorene (PF), poly(fluorene-co-phenylene), poly(p-phenyleneethynylene) (PPE), poly (fluorenyl divinylene) (PFV), poly(benzothiadiazole) and poly (pheneylenevinylene) (PPV) [21⿿23] are also explored for bioimaging applications. However, some ionic side-chains in the conjugated polymers can lead to non-specific interactions with cells and tissues. There are also some drawbacks with polymer conjugates such as limited half-life in vivo and early burst release of drug.

Stimuli responsive polymers are widely used for biomedical applications because they respond to external stimuli such as pH, temperature and redox potential. They act as programmable drug delivery vehicles where the release of the loaded drug can be controlled by the external stimulus [24]. Among different types of stimulus responsive polymers, pH responsive polymers such as micelles, liposomes and hydrogels are very important in drug delivery [25⿿27] The pH sensitivity of these polymers arises due to the ionization of the pendant carboxylic acid or amino groups with pKa values ranging from 3 to 11 [28].

Degradable polymers based on α-Amino acid are extensively used for biomedical applications owing to their versatile chemical functionalities, high biocompatibility and degradability [29]. Hence, the development of α-amino acid containing degradable polymers with innate fluorescence can become an ideal substitute for the currently used fluorophores for different biomedical applications. There are very limited reports available on amino acid containing degradable fluorescent polymers. Yang etal reported an aliphatic α-amino acid containing degradable fluorescent polymer based on citric acid and amino acid [30].The innate fluorescence behavior of this polymer is attributed to the reaction of citric acid with amino acid leading to the development of cyclic structures responsible for fluorescence behavior. Our group has reported the synthesis of degradable fluorescent polymer based on α-amino acid (Glycine) and biocompatile polymer PEG. This fluorescent polymer exhibited excitation dependent fluorescence behavior with innate near IR emissions [31]. The study also explored the intriguing innate fluorescence behavior associated with the condensed polyesters of PEG with different monomers such as maleic acid and glycine.

Nanogels are chemically or physically cross linked polymeric nanostructures endowed with properties such as high water absorptivity, rapid response to external stimuli, stimuli responsive swelling, high dispersion stability, network structure permeability, mechanical strength and biocompatibility. These properties make it appropriate for executing both drug delivery and imaging [32⿿35]. Nanogels endowed with near IR emissions are extensively used for bioimaging applications. Chen etal reported a near IR emitting gold cluster-polyacrylic acid based nanogel for bioimaging [36]. Recently, Park etal reported a sunflower type nanogel carrying quantum dots for tracing human mesenchymal stem cells [37]. These reported nanogels are incorporated with an external imaging agent such as gold nanoparticles, quantum dots and cyanine dyes for accomplishing the imaging. A fluorescent boronate hydrogel with phenylboronic acid dye was reported for sensing of Zn2+ ions in drinking water [38]. In the case of boronate hydrogel, two different types of fluorescent dyes were used for getting fluorescent characteristics. Reports on self-fluorescent nanogel with innate fluorescence are rare. Abietane based self-fluorescent biocompatible nanogel has been reported [39]; however, the major drawback with the abietane based nanogel is that it has only one emission at 500nm, in visible region at which chance of tissue auto fluorescence is very high. Hence, the development of self-fluorescent aliphatic polyester based nanogel with low cytotoxicity and strong fluorescence in near IR region is highly desirable for bioimaging. In the present study, we report a novel aliphatic degradable stimuli responsive polymeric nanogel based on [PEG-poly (propylene fumarate)-citric acid-glycine] and N, N dimethyl amino ethylmethacrylate (DMEMA) endowed with innate near IR fluorescent capability for drug delivery and bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Maleic anhydride, 1,2- propylene glycol, polyethylene glycol (PEG, MW: 300), citric acid, N,N dimethyl amino ethylmethacrylate (DMEMA), acetone and methanol were purchased from Merck (India). Ammonium persulfate (APS), tetra methyl ethylene diamine (TEMED) and glycine were obtained from Sigma (Bangalore, India). Dialysis bag (500D molecular weight cut-off), Dulbecco⿿s modified eagle⿿s medium (DMEM) and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Invitrogen (India). MTT (3-(4,5-dimethyl thiazol-2-yl)-2,5 diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay kit was purchased from Hi Media (India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of stimuli responsive self fluorescent nanogel
ce:section.ce:para: Initially a modified citric acid containing photoluminescent comacromer (C-PLM) was synthesized with small modifications reported previously by us [31]. Briefly, PEG-300 (7.5g) was refluxed with 0.05M carboxy terminated poly (propylene fumarate) (CT-PPF) (12.5g) and citric acid (2.62g) under nitrogen atmosphere for 15min at 180°C. After melting of the reaction mixture, glycine (1g) was added and the reaction was continued for further 100min. The comacromer formed was a brownish coloured resin. The comacromer was then subjected to dialysis through a dialysis membrane (500 D) for 3days to completely remove the unreacted monomers. The comacromer was then lyophilized and stored at 4°C.

The comacromer was analysed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer. FT-IR spectra were recorded in the region of 400⿿4000cm⿿1 using a spectrometer (Jasco, FT/IR-4200, Easton, USA), with resin smear placed between KBr plates. Data acquisition and analysis was performed using JASCO's proprietary Spectra Manager TM II cross-platform software. The nuclear magnetic resonance spectrum of the C-PLM was recorded using a Bruker 500 MHZ spectrometer (Bruker Biospin, Billerica, MA) with CDCl3 as solvent. The molecular weight of C-PLM was determined using a gel permeation chromatography (GPC) system (Waters) equipped with a 600 series pump, refractive index detector (Waters 2414) and styragel columns (HR-5E/4E/2/0.5) connected in series. Briefly, 20μl of 1% polymer solution in THF was injected into the system with the mobile phase (THF) set at a flow rate of 1ml/min. Polystyrene standards (Mp: 100000, 9130 and 162) were used for relative calibration.

The hydrogel was prepared using C-PLM. Briefly, C-PLM (molecular weight Mn 1335, Mw 3171) (0.3g) and DMEMA (30μl) were added to a culture tube and gently mixed to get a homogenous paste. The content was further mixed with equal volumes of redox initiators, APS (50μl) and TEMED (50μl) to allow the crosslinking. The hydrogel was coded as C-PLM-HG. Nanogel was prepared using C-PLM as follows; briefly 40mg of C-PLM was dissolved in 5ml water to form a uniform homogenous solution. To this solution, DMEMA (10μl), was added followed by APS (30μl) and TEMED. The beaker containing the above solution was then sonicated for 30min. After sonication a slight turbidity was observed indicating the crosslinking into nanogel. The nanogel was coded as C-PLM-NG.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Swelling and morphology of gels
ce:section.ce:para:
For the study of pH responsive swelling of hydrogels, the freeze-dried hydrogel discs of known weights were soaked in 20ml PBS solution having different pH values such as pH 5.5, 6, 7.4 and 8 for 96h. At different time period, the samples were removed and the solvent on the surface of the swollen hydrogel was blotted, the wet weight was measured. The degree of swelling of the hydrogel was calculated using the equation
Swelling Degree
( % )
=
(
Weight of the swollen hydrogel ⿿ Weight of the dry hydrogel
)
Weight of the dry hydrogel
ÿ 100

The morphology of the C-PLM-NG was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips CM12 STEM, Netherlands). For TEM analysis, the C-PLM-NG was dispersed in water ultrasonically to form dilute suspension. Then, one drop was deposited on the carbon-coated copper. The grid was air-dried for 2days at room temperature and then examined.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of photoluminescent properties of C-PLM and its gels
ce:section.ce:para: Initially the photoluminescence spectrum of the C-PLM comacromer under aqueous condition was recorded using JASCO FP-8200 spectro fluorimeter at room temperature. C-PLM solution (1mg/ml) was loaded inside a quartz cuvette having a path length of 10mm. The excitation and emission slit width were kept at 1.5nm. The photostability of the C-PLM comacromer was evaluated under aqueous condition by continuously exciting the C-PLM at the excitation wavelength of 420nm in a spectrofluorimeter and monitored the change in emission intensity at 525nm.

The quantum yield of the C-PLM was measured by the Williams method. C-PLM solution having concentration 2mg/ml was prepared. For the measurement of quantum yield, the emission wavelength of the C-PLM was matched with that of reference dye (anthracene). The C-PLM solution was scanned at different excitation wavelengths. The optimum excitation wavelength, 340nm that generated the highest emission intensity at 420nm was selected. Anthracene (quantum yield=0.27 in ethanol) which has excitation/emission 340/420nm was used as a standard. UV⿿vis absorbance spectrum was collected with the same solution and the absorbance at the excitation wavelength was noted. A series of test solutions with different concentrations was prepared whose absorbance lies in the range 0.01-0.1.The fluorescence spectrum was recorded for the same solution using a 10mm fluorescence cuvette. Integrated fluorescence intensity was then calculated and plotted a graph of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance. The quantum yield of the nanogel was calculated according to the equation ɸX=ɸST (Grad X/Grad ST) (ƿX/ƿST)2where the subscripts ST and X denote standard and test respectively, ɸ is the fluorescence quantum yield, Grad the gradient from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance and ƿ is the refractive index of the solvent. The C-PLM was dissolved in distilled water (pH 7.4, 25°c) and anthracene was dissolved in ethanol. The slit width was kept same for both standard and the test sample. Absorbance was measured using JASCO V-550 spectrophotometer.

The solid state photoluminescence spectra of C-PLM-HG were recorded using freeze-dried C-PLM-HG sample. A fluorolog jobin yvon spectro fluorimeter was used for recording the spectra. The time resolved fluorescence experiments were performed using an IBH nanosecond single-photon counting. A 440nm nano-LED excitation source and a Hamamatsu C4878⿿02micro channel plate (MCP) detector were used. The photoluminescence spectra of the C-PLM-NG was recorded for excitations from 460nm to 600nm under aqueous condition. The freeze-dried C-PLM-NG sample was dispersed in distilled water (1mg/ml) and used for recording the spectra at room temperature. A FP-8200 spectro fluorimeter was used. The images of NIR emission of the C-PLM-NG from 660nm to 700nm were captured with the excitation wavelength, 535nm using different emission filters using Xenogen IVIS optical imaging system. Briefly, 100μl of nanogel (2mg/ml) was loaded on to a 96 well plate and placed inside the optical imaging system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug loading and release profiles of nanogel
ce:section.ce:para:
The drug loading and release profiles of the nanogel were studied using anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) as a model drug. For drug loading, DOX (1mg) was dissolved in the aqueous phase and added to the reaction mixture containing C-PLM (40mg) and DMEMA (10μl). The nanogel loaded with DOX was prepared by adding DOX along with C-PLM and DMEMA as described in 2.2. The DOX- loaded nanogel dispersion (pH 7.4) was then subjected to dialysis through a 500D MW cut-off dialysis bag for 72h to completely remove the unloaded DOX from the nanogel. A small portion of DOX-loaded nanogel was taken and measured the fluorescent emission using a fluorescent microplate reader (Tecan) with ex: 460nm and em: 595nm. The drug loading level was determined using a concentration versus fluorescence emission calibration curve of DOX.
Drug loading efficiency
( % )
=
Weight of loaded DOX
Weight of DOX in the nanogel suspension
ÿ 100

Drug release profile was evaluated at different pH through dialysis method. DOX-loaded nanogel suspension (5ml) was placed within a dialysis bag (MW cut-off 1000 D), followed by dialysis against phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution (50ml) having different pH values. At prescribed time intervals, 1ml of external buffer solution was withdrawn and replaced with fresh equivalent volume of medium. Concentration of DOX was determined using a calibration curve concentration versus fluorescence emission of DOX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cytotoxicity of the nanogel
ce:section.ce:para:
The cytotoxicity of nanogel was evaluated using L929 fibroblast cell line. Briefly, cells were seeded at a density of 1ÿ105 cells/well (200μl, 96-well tissue culture plate) and cultured in complete DMEM medium at 37°C in 5% CO2 for 24h. Then cells were incubated with different concentrations of nanogel (50⿿450μg/ml). After 24h incubation, 20μl MTT solution (5mg/ml, PBS) was added and the plate was incubated for another 4h at 37°C. DMSO (200μl) was then added to each well and the plate was placed in an orbital shaker for 15min at 200rpm to dissolve the formazan crystals formed. The absorbance at 570nm was determined using a UV/Vis microplate reader (Tecan). The percentage of viable cells was calculated using the equation,
Cell Viability =
[
Abs
]
sample
[
Abs
]
Control
ÿ 100.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hemolysis test
ce:section.ce:para:
In vitro hemolytic potential of C-PLM-NG was investigated according to ISO 10993-4:2002 standard. Blood from the human volunteer was collected into the anticoagulant, citrate phosphate dextrose adenine (CPDA). The test sample (1ml) was taken into the three wells of six well polystyrene dishes in triplicates and 2ml blood was added. One ml solution was aspirated immediately for initial analysis and the remaining solution was agitated using an Environ shaker thermo stated at 35±2°C for 30min at 75±5rpm. Four wells of empty polystyrene culture dishes were exposed with blood as reference. The blood samples were centrifuged at 4000rpm for 15min and platelet poor plasma was aspirated. Percentage of hemolysis was calculated by measuring the total hemoglobin using automatic hematology analyzer (Sysmex-K 4500). The free hemoglobin liberated in to the plasma before and after exposure to blood was measured in each sample using diode array spectrophotometer. Hemolysis (%) was calculated using the formula.
Hemolysis
( % )
=
Free Hb
Total Hb / 1000
ÿ 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cellular uptake of the nanogel
ce:section.ce:para: Cellular uptake of the nanogel was evaluated by using HeLa cell line. Briefly, the cells were seeded at a density of 1ÿ105 cells/well (200μl, 96-well tissue culture plate) and cultured in complete DMEM medium at 37°C in 5% CO2 for 24h. The medium was then removed and 2wt% of nanogel in PBS was added and incubated for 4h at 37°C. Cells were then washed with PBS twice and observed the cellular uptake under a fluorescent microscope (optica, ITALY) at a magnification of 40ÿ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on in vivo bioimaging and biodistribution in mice
ce:section.ce:para: For studies on bioimaging and biodistribution, Swiss albino mice with body weight range of 35⿿40g were anesthetized with xylazine and ketamine. The back of the mice was clipped and epilated for subcutaneous injection. Briefly, 0.25ml of C-PLM-NG prepared in PBS solution with concentration 2mg/ml was injected subcutaneously in the back of mice and then imaged with Xenogen IVIS spectrum optical imaging system. Initially to choose the excitation wavelength which offers the emission at near IR region with relatively higher intensity, different excitations (430, 465, 505 and 535nm) were used and emission wavelengths ranging from visible to near IR (540, 580, 610, 660, 680 and 700nm) were collected using filters. As the emission at near IR region has relatively higher intensity for the excitation wavelength of 535nm, capturing of broad Near IR emission was carried out with the excitation wavelength, 535nm using different emission filters.

For biodistribution studies, 0.25ml of C-PLM-NG in PBS solution (2mg/ml) was injected through the lateral tail vein of the mice and then imaged at 3h and 6h with Xenogen IVIS spectrum optical imaging system with the excitation wavelength, 535nm. Also ex vivo imaging of organs, urinary bladder, liver, kidney and heart etc from the carcass of the mice was carried out at 3h and 24h post injection after opening the abdomen and thorax. Histopathological evaluation of the heart, liver and kidney was also carried out. For histopathology studies, formalin-fixed tissue samples were dehydrated through ascending grades of alcohol, cleared in xylene and impregnated with paraffin wax. Leica RM2255⿢ microtome was used for making tissue sections of 4μm thickness from paraffin tissue blocks. The sections were then stained with haematoxylin and eosin dyes (H&E) for microscopic observation. The application of the present nanogel for lymph node imaging was also studied. For this studies, the back of the mice was clipped and epilated before imaging. Then, 0.1ml of C-PLM-NG was injected into the ipsilateral foot pad of the mice. After 3h, the animal was imaged. All the animal experiments were conducted after getting permission from Institutional Animal Ethics Committee (IAEC).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: All experimental data were analyzed by Student⿿s t tests to evaluate the significance of differences between the sub groups considered. P values<0.05 were statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Preparation and characterisation of modified photoluminescent comacromer (C-PLM)
ce:section.ce:para: A pH responsive polymeric nanogel with excitation dependent fluorescence (EDF) behavior was designed using a modified citric acid containing α-amino acid based polyester comacromer (C-PLM) and N, N dimethyl amino ethylmethacrylate (DMEMA). The scheme for the synthesis of modified comacromer (C-PLM) and nanogel is narrated in Fig. 1 . FTIR spectrum of C-PLM (Fig. 2 a) reveals peaks at 3435cm⿿1 (-OH stretching vibrations of PEG), 3083cm⿿1 (CH stretching vibrations of CHCH bonds of CT-PPF), 2918cm⿿1 (CH stretching vibrations of CH2 groups PEG and citric acid), 1713cm⿿1 (ester bonds of CT-PPF), 1645cm⿿1 (CC stretching of CHCH- bond of CT-PPF), 1304cm⿿1 (CC and CO stretching of ester linkages of CT-PPF),1118cm⿿1 (ether CO group stretching for PEG) and 1039cm⿿1 (CN stretching vibrations of glycine) suggesting the formation of C-PLM. The structure of the synthesized C-PLM was confirmed through proton NMR spectral analyses of C-PLM (Fig. 2b).1H NMR spectrum of C-PLM shows peaks at 6.8ppm (from the fumarate double bonds CHCH of polypropylene fumarate), 4.7ppm (NH groups of glycine), 3.4ppm (CH 2 groups from PEG) and peaks at 2.75ppm (-CH 2 from citric acid). The FT-IR and NMR analyses substantiate the structure of the C-PLM. The molecular weight data of C-PLM are Mn 1335, Mw 3171 and polydispersity index 2.38 revealing the macromer nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of photoluminescence of the C-PLM comacromer
ce:section.ce:para: Excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence (EDF) is an interesting phenomenon exhibited by carbon dots, carbon nanotubes, graphene etc in polar solvents [40⿿42]. EDF enabled fluorophore can be excited with different wavelength to generate fluorescence red-shifted from visible to near IR. Unlike the conventional fluorophores such as organic dyes and quantum dots in which fluorescence tuning can be accomplished only through composition or size change, fluorophores exhibiting EDF can be tuned from visible to near IR region without the change of composition or size [43]. These unique features of EDF render them wide applications such as bioimaging, biosensing and FRET ratiometric sensing.

Surface passivation of carbon dots with different groups such as organic, polymeric and inorganic materials on carbon dots was resorted to optimize their optical properties. However, these surface passivating agents often have toxicity issues [44,45]. For safer bioimaging, it is mandatory that the imaging material after performing bioimaging function should be metabolized or excreted from the body. Hence the development of safer and biodegradable polymeric system with innate EDF phenomenon is highly relevant for bioimaging applications. In the present study, the modified α-amino acid containing degradable comacromer (C-PLM) was synthesized. The ester linkages present in the poly(propylene fumarate) segments imparts degradability to the synthesized C-PLM to enable the clearance from the body. The degradation mainly takes place via the hydrolytic cleavage of the ester linkages of the polymer backbone as narrated elsewhere [46].

In the present studies, C-PLM exhibited EDF behaviour; the photoluminescence spectra of the C-PLM comacromer in aqueous condition reveals emissions ranging from visible to near IR range, 510⿿718nm for excitation in the range of 400⿿640nm in aqueous condition (Fig. 3 a). The study of fluorescence lifetime of C-PLM reveals triexponential decay pathway with major components having lifetime 7ns (Fig. 3b) which is similar to that of color-switchable fluorescent carbon dots [47]. C-PLM also exhibited photostability without quenching even under continuous irradiation for a period of 30min (Fig. 3c) when compared to organic dye like cy5 which has drawbacks like photo bleaching while using for bioimaging [48]. Quantum yield is another important property for any fluorophores; C-PLM has quantum yield of 0.5 (50%) in water (Fig. 3d), which is very high when compared to that of molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (9%) [49]. C-PLM exhibited Stokes shift of 120nm (Fig. 3e) which is higher than that of organic dye, perylene (40nm) [50]. The observed EDF fluorescent behavior of C-PLM can be attributed to the n-Ͽ* interactions of nucleophilic oxygen atoms of PEG with the multiple number of electrophilic ester carbonyl groups (O⿯CO interactions) of the comacromer as reported elsewhere [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies of photoluminescence of the C-PLM based hydrogel and nanogel
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent characteristic of hydrogel and nanogel was studied. Interestingly, the hydrogel C-PLM-HG exhibits fluorescence as displayed with the formation of fluorescent hydrogel and gel doc image given in Fig. 4 a. In order to explore the fluorescent nature of C-PLM-HG, the fluorescence spectra of C-PLM-HG was recorded in solid state, which shows emission peaks around 660nm irrespective of excitation wavelength when excited at 480nm, 500nm and 520nm (Fig. 4b). The EDF characteristics are not observed predominantly in the solid C-PLM-HG. The EDF characteristics are observed mostly in materials solvated in polar liquid such as with graphene oxide [43]. The absence of EDF characteristics in the solid C-PLM-HG is due to the lack of solvato-chromic shift as reported by Lakowicz [51].

The studies on fluorescence characteristics of C-PLM NG nanogel in aqueous condition reveal emissions red-shifted from 523nm to 700nm for excitations from 460nm to 600nm (Fig. 4c) with reduced intensity with increase in excitation ascertaining the EDF characteristics [52]. The fluorescent spectrum of C-PLM NG shows broad spectrum emission profile at near IR region (660nm⿿700nm).The broad Near IR emission was further diagnosed by capturing optical images with different filters using the excitation wavelength of 535nm (Fig. 4d). Cheng et al. reported similar emissions in the visible region from 450nm to 600nm for excitation at 310⿿530nm for carbon dots [53]; however, in comparison with luminescent carbon dots, the present C-PLM-NG has fluorescence at the Near IR region (700nm) where the chance of tissue autofluorescence is negligible. The display of emissions of C-PLM NG nanogel red-shifted from 523nm to 700nm for excitations from 460nm to 600nm in aqueous condition, in contrast to that observed with C-PLM-HG in solid state, is attributed to the solvation process during the florescence emission. With excitation of C-PLM NG nanogel in aqueous condition, the salvation relaxation process occurs on the same time of florescence emission, which induces the excitation dependent red-shift as reported by Cushing et al. [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Swelling and morphology of gels
ce:section.ce:para: The studies on swelling of C-PLM-HG at different pH (Fig. 4e) reveal pH responsive swelling behavior; it exhibited more swelling at acidic pH when compared in normal pH which can be attributed to the presence of the crosslinker DMAEMA with tertiary amino group with pka value of 7.8 [54]. The pH sensitive swelling of C-PLM-HG may be relevant for cancer therapy, since cancer cells have acidic environment. Nanogel, C-PLM-NG exhibits high dispersion stability, which is attributed to PEG chains present on the C-PLM as reported by Otsuka et al. [55]. The pH sensitive swelling property of the present nanogel is visualized through TEM analysis. C-PLM-NG exhibits different size depending on the pH of the solution (Fig. 4f). The nanogel has particle size around 100nm and 180nm at pH 7.4 (physiological) and 5.5 (intracellular acidic condition of cancer cells) respectively. Further, the dynamic light scattering measurements of the nanogel reveal particle size around 115nm at pH 7.4 and 190nm at pH 5.5 substantiating the pH sensitive swelling of C-PLM-NG (Fig. 4g). The observed pH sensitive swelling behavior of C-PLM-NG can be attributed to the tertiary amino group of the crosslinker that undergoes protonation and causes subsequent swelling in acidic environment due to increase in the ion osmotic pressure with polymer solvation [56]. The observed pH sensitive swelling behavior of C-PLM-NG is comparable with that of pH sensitive nanogels prepared with PEG having polymerizable vinyl benzyl groups and DMEMA [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of cytocompatibility, hemocompatibility, drug release and cellular uptake of nanogel
ce:section.ce:para: The studies on cytocompatibility of the nanogel C-PLM-NG reveal good cell viability for different concentrations ranging from 500 to 5000μg (Fig. 5 a).The observed cell viability for the different concentrations of nanogel is higher than those of wavelength-tunable photoluminescent carbon dots [58]. Further, the study on hemolysis of the nanogel reveals very low hemolysis, 0.02%, for the concentration of 2mg/ml in comparison with 0.015 for control. The hemolytic percentage is very much lesser than that of the acceptable limit of 5% for biomaterials according to ISO standards. Thus the very low hemolytic data for C-PLM-NG suggests the hemocompatibility of this material. DOX is loaded inside the nanogel during the preparation of nanogel. The drug loading efficiency is found to be 80% for C-PLM-NG. The higher DOX loading efficiency observed for the C-PLM-NG is comparable to that of bioreducible heparin based nanogels [59].The studies on pH sensitive swelling of nanogel and release of DOX reveal 15% release in normal physiological pH; but in intracellular acidic pH 5.5, the release of DOX significantly increases reaching up to 48% (Fig. 5b). From the release profile of DOX, it is evident that the present nanogel exhibits favorable pH dependent drug release kinetics. The observed release of DOX from C-PLM-NG is comparable to that of chitin-poly (L-lactic acid) composite nanogel reported for liver cancer [60]. MTT assay on Hela cell line with the nanogel loaded with DOX reveal low cell viability (Fig. 5c); the observed decrease in cell viability is attributed to the release of this anticancer drug from the C-PLM-NG. This result suggests the good therapeutic potential of C-PLM-NG for cancer treatment. The inherent EDF characteristics associated with C-PLM NG enable cellular imaging of the nanogel with Hela cell (Fig. 5d) when excited at two different excitations 488nm and 561nm. The observed fluorescence imaging of Hela cells is comparable with that of carbon dots derived from solanum tuberosum [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo fluorescence bioimaging studies on mice
ce:section.ce:para: The efficacy of C-PLM-NG for bioimaging is assessed using mice model. The excitation wavelength which offers the emission at near IR region with relatively higher intensity was chosen. The images of the subcutaneously injected nanogel for excitations (430, 465, 505 and 535nm) and emissions (540, 580, 610, 660, 680 and 700nm) which are collected using filters are given in Fig. 6 a and 6b. Since the excitation wavelength of 535nm offers emission at near IR region with relatively higher intensity, the images for the Near IR emission was captured using different emission filters. The processed images captured for the near IR emission at 660nm⿿700nm for the excitation at 535nm are also given in Fig. 6b. With the possibility of imaging the near IR emission using cut-off filters, biodistribution and clearance mechanism from the body could be evaluated without any auto fluorescence. Moreover, as the emission of the present nanogel lies in the Near IR region, deep tissue penetration and imaging is highly feasible with the present C-PLM-NG.

It is necessary that any biomaterial after performing its intended function should be metabolized or excreted from the body without creating any toxicological problems. Hence biodistribution and clearance mechanism of C-PLM-NG from the body of mice was also studied. C-PLM-NG injected in the mice through tail vein reveals a spot like signal from liver region after 3h post injection (Fig. 6c). After 6h post injection, signal from liver region disappeared and a strong visible signal appeared from urinary bladder and urine. This observation suggests that the primary excretion pathway of the nanogel is through urine and bile.

Ex vivo imaging of organs with C-PLM-NG on mice carried out at 3h and 24h of post injection offered further insight on biodistribution (Fig. 7 a). After 3h post injection, imaging of the carcass after opening the abdomen and thorax with organs in situ reveals signals from liver especially from the gall bladder as well as urinary bladder. At this time, the dissected organs liver, kidney and heart were also imaged. Very strong fluorescent signal appeared from kidney and liver while no fluorescence from heart. The high accumulation of C-PLM-NG on excretory organs, liver and kidney suggests that C-PLM-NG can be cleared easily by the body. Ex vivo imaging of the carcass at 24h post injection revealed no signals from the animal body suggesting that the material is eliminated from the animal without any toxicological concerns. Histopathological evaluation of the heart, liver and kidney revealed normal tissue morphology without any significant change or signs of necrosis (Fig. 7b). Liver section showed normal sinusoids, portal triads and central vein. The heart section has visible striations in the myocardium with centrally placed nuclei. Both the cortical and medullary areas in the kidneys appeared normal with no visible abnormalities to glomeruli, tubules and blood vessels histologically. Thus histopathological analysis revealed that the C-PLM-NG does not elicit any acute toxicological response in vivo.

Lymph node imaging is an important tool for differentiating the benign and malignant nodes. Though several approaches are developed, there are certain deficiencies in those methods. Therefore, lymph node imaging with fluorescent nanogels has greater relevance for cancer diagnosis. With the present studies, the tracking of migration of nanogel to axillary lymph nodes of mice is accomplished with the fluorescence from axillary lymph nodes as shown in Fig. 8 . This suggests the potential of C-PLM-NG for sentimental lymph node mapping, which is vital in cancer biopsy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: A novel aliphatic polymeric nanogel (C-PLM-NG) with innate Near IR emissions is developed. C-PLM-NG exhibited EDF phenomenon with stimuli (pH) responsive swelling behavior in aqueous condition. C-PLM NG exhibits emissions from 523nm to 700nm for excitations from 460nm to 600nm in aqueous condition ascertaining the EDF characteristics. Imaging the near IR emission was accomplished using cut-off filters with excitation at 535nm. The studies on in vitro anticancer drug loading and release revealed high drug loading and pH responsive drug release profile. The present nanogel is cytocompatible and hemocompatible. The studies on internalization of DOX-loaded C-PLM-NG in Hela cells demonstrated the lysis of cancer cells. The in vitro imaging of cell-encapsulated nanogel was ascertained with fluorescence emissions at two different excitations. The studies on biodistribution and clearance mechanism of C-PLM-NG from the body of mice reveal bioimaging capability and safety of the present nanogel. In the current area of nanomedicine, self-fluorescent polymeric nanogel systems with innate theranostic capabilities are rarely reported. The new nanogel has immense scope as theranotisc tool for cancer therapy due to the following reasons: (i) The nanogel was synthesized from biocompatible monomers; hence issues related to cytotoxicity currently faced by many existing theranostic agents do not emerge. (ii) The present nanogel possesses appreciable drug loading and pH responsive release characteristics. (iii) This polymeric nanogel exhibits EDF behavior and favours Near IR imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Director and Head, BMT Wing, SCTIMST for providing the facilities to carry out this work. The authors acknowledge the help provided by Remya K and Dr. Shivaram Selvam during the studies. Authors also acknowledge Dr. Jayasree R S, Ms. Lekshmi V Nair, Dr. T.V. Anilkumar and Dr. Geetha for providing the animal imaging and histopathological facilities during the studies. V.M.V acknowledges the financial support from Sree Chitra Tirunal Institute for Medical Sciences & Technology for Junior Research Fellowship.
